ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Guobaorou or Harbin-style sweet and sour pork; 'pork dish' (FOODON_03302757) used as the closest defensible broad match.
ontology.foodex2_description: Pork, cooked — used as the closest FoodEx2 anchor for a battered, deep-fried, sauced pork dish; no specific Guobaorou code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not contain a specific code for Harbin-style sweet and sour pork (Guobaorou). A0CKP (cooked pork) is the closest defensible anchor. Dish-level FoodEx2 codes for Chinese regional preparations are not available.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code found for Guobaorou or Harbin pineapple sweet and sour pork. SNOMED CT does not index regional Chinese dish preparations at this specificity.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Harbin Pineapple Sweet and Sour Pork is approximately 200 g, representing a shared side dish portion for one person in a multi-dish Chinese meal.

nutrient_source.title: Harbin Pineapple Sweet And Sour Pork 哈尔滨凤梨锅包肉 — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Harbin Pineapple Sweet And Sour Pork (cr_id G00010914). The HPB record reports carbohydrate and sugar as 0.0 g/100g, which appears to reflect only the pork component and not the full dish with sweet-sour sauce, pineapple, and cornstarch batter. This discrepancy is documented in notes. Saturated fat is not reported in the HPB record; estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for pork loin, cooked (NDB 10062) as a secondary reference for fatty acid profile.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of sweet and sour pork — analogous estimate based on battered deep-fried pork with sweet sauce; reference: University of Sydney GI Database entry for battered/crumbed meat dishes and sweet sauces (GI ~60–70 range)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Harbin Pineapple Sweet and Sour Pork (Guobaorou) in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on analogous deep-fried battered pork dishes with sweet sauces from the University of Sydney GI Database. The cornstarch batter and sweet-sour sauce (ketchup, sugar, pineapple) contribute moderate-to-high GI carbohydrates. The HPB source records carbohydrate as 0.0 g/100g (reflecting pork component only); the actual dish carbohydrate content is estimated at 8–15 g/100g when sauce and batter are included. Glycemic load per serving is calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (65 × 16 g) / 100 ≈ 10.4 for a 200 g serving using estimated 8 g/100g carbohydrate; however, because the HPB-reported carbohydrate is 0.0 g, GL is set to 0.0 per the source data with the caveat documented in notes.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 1050 mg/100g — nearly double the 600 mg/100g upper threshold for hypertension-sensitive foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit Harbin Pineapple Sweet and Sour Pork; a 200 g serving delivers approximately 2100 mg sodium, representing 91% of the WHO daily sodium limit of 2300 mg, and the combination of soy sauce, ketchup, and added salt in the sweet-sour sauce makes sodium reduction without recipe modification impossible.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Deep-fried cornstarch batter and sweet-sour sauce with added sugar and pineapple create a high glycaemic load despite the HPB record showing 0 g carbohydrate (pork component only)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this dish; the actual dish contains significant refined carbohydrates from cornstarch batter and added sugars from the sweet-sour sauce (estimated 8–15 g/100g), producing a medium-to-high glycaemic index of approximately 65, and the deep-frying method further impairs postprandial glucose control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Combination of high sodium (1050 mg/100g), deep-frying-derived saturated and trans fats, and high cholesterol (89 mg/100g) creates a multi-pathway cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid Harbin Pineapple Sweet and Sour Pork; the deep-frying process elevates saturated fat and generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that promote arterial inflammation, while the extreme sodium load (1050 mg/100g) directly elevates blood pressure and the cholesterol content (89 mg/100g) contributes to LDL burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates AGEs that worsen joint inflammation; pork is a red meat source of arachidonic acid; sodium at 1050 mg/100g far exceeds the 400 mg/100g RA threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish; deep-frying generates pro-inflammatory AGEs, pork provides arachidonic acid that amplifies inflammatory cytokine production, bell peppers are nightshades that may exacerbate symptoms in sensitive individuals, and the extreme sodium load further promotes systemic inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Harbin Pineapple Sweet and Sour Pork (哈尔滨凤梨锅包肉, Guobaorou) is a classic Northeastern Chinese (Dongbei) dish originating from Harbin, Heilongjiang Province. It consists of thinly sliced pork loin coated in a thick cornstarch batter, deep-fried until golden and crispy, then tossed in a glossy sweet-sour sauce made from ketchup, rice vinegar, sugar, soy sauce, and pineapple chunks, with bell peppers and onion adding colour and texture. The dish delivers a characteristic contrast of crispy exterior and tender pork interior, with a tangy-sweet sauce that coats each piece. Nutritionally, the HPB Singapore database records 171 kcal, 19.1 g protein, and 10.5 g fat per 100 g, with sodium at an extremely high 1050 mg/100g. The carbohydrate value of 0.0 g/100g in the HPB record reflects only the pork component and does not capture the significant carbohydrate contribution from the cornstarch batter and sweet-sour sauce; the actual dish is estimated to contain 8–15 g carbohydrate per 100 g. Clinically, this dish is unsuitable for individuals with hypertension (extreme sodium load), Type 2 Diabetes (high-GI batter and added sugars), cardiovascular disease (deep-frying, saturated fat, cholesterol), and rheumatoid arthritis (AGEs, red meat, nightshades). It is served as a shared main dish in Chinese restaurant settings, typically at lunch or dinner.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values (energy, protein, fat, sodium, potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, cholesterol) sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010914, confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved source for Singapore-served dishes. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central cooked pork loin as a secondary reference. The carbohydrate anomaly (0.0 g/100g) is a known limitation of the HPB record and is fully documented.
notes: ZERO MACRO JUSTIFICATION (g4): carbohydrate_g is reported as 0.0 and sugar_g is reported as 0.0 in the HPB source record. These values appear to reflect only the pork component of the dish and do not capture carbohydrates from the cornstarch batter, sweet-sour sauce (ketchup, sugar), or pineapple. The HPB record is preserved as-is per source fidelity rules; however, the actual dish carbohydrate content is estimated at 8–15 g/100g. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 per HPB source; this is plausible for the pork component but likely underestimates fibre from pineapple and vegetables in the full dish. These zeros are confirmed zero per HPB source record and documented here as required. NEGATIVE SENTINEL VALUES (g3): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, vitamin panel, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and moisture data for this specific Harbin-style sweet and sour pork preparation are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as a composite dish entry as of 2026-04-16. SATURATED FAT: saturated_fat_g estimated at 3.2 g/100g based on USDA FoodData Central entry for pork loin, cooked (NDB 10062), proportioned to the reported fat content of 10.5 g/100g; this is an approximation and may not reflect the full dish fat profile including frying oil absorption. GI CAVEAT: GI of 65 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Guobaorou exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GL per serving is set to 0.0 per HPB-reported carbohydrate of 0.0 g; actual GL using estimated 8 g/100g carbohydrate for a 200 g serving would be approximately 10.4. HALAL STATUS: This dish contains pork and is not halal.
